blurb: Oracle said JDK 27, due in September 2026, will be the final release to ship a build for Intel-based Macs, ending x86 macOS support in subsequent versions. Enterprises still running Intel MacBooks in their developer fleets now face a hard cutoff for current Java runtimes, pushing migration to Apple Silicon or pinning workloads to JDK 27's support window.

title: Microsoft admits a default Windows 11 feature is quietly gobbling up massive disk space
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a bug in the Windows 11 Capability Access Manager that let its CapabilityAccessManager.db-wal file balloon disk usage by as much as 70GB, and is rolling out a fix in the KB5095093 C-release update for versions 24H2 and 25H2. The patch ships as a normal rollout to all users, alongside a gradual File Explorer speedup for mounting disk images.

title: Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron sued over alleged DRAM price fixing amid record memory costs — lawsuit claims coordinated HBM shift was cover to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 production
blurb: Three plaintiffs filed a California class action on June 25th accusing Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron, which together control roughly 90% of the DRAM market, of coordinating to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 output and inflate prices the complaint says rose about 700% over four years. The suit invokes Section 1 of the Sherman Act and frames the industry-wide pivot to high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators as cover for cutting commodity supply, seeking treble damages and an injunction.

title: FCC escalates China crackdown by closing equipment import loophole
blurb: The FCC moved to bar pre-2022-authorized network equipment from Covered List vendors like Huawei and ZTE, closing a loophole that let barred gear remain in U.S. critical infrastructure and public-safety systems. The rules take effect in July and extend Chairman Brendan Carr's China crackdown, which already covers blanket carrier authorizations, foreign-made routers, and subsea cable provisions.

title: AI Overviews in Drive now available on mobile
blurb: Google made AI Overviews in Drive available in the Android and iOS apps, extending the Gemini-powered search summaries that reached general availability on the web in April. The feature is on by default wherever Gemini for Workspace in Drive is enabled, covering Business Standard and Plus, Enterprise Standard and Plus, and Google AI Pro and Ultra tiers, with a rollout across English and 28 additional languages over the coming weeks.

title: Ask Gemini in Drive now available on mobile
blurb: Google extended Ask Gemini in Drive to the Android and iOS apps, adding the mobile version of the immersive assistant it made generally available on the web in April. The feature supports multi-turn conversations grounded in specific files and folders, honors existing access permissions, DLP, and IRM controls, and is included at no extra cost for Business and Enterprise Standard and Plus customers with Gemini for Workspace enabled.

title: ServiceNow, Accenture team up to target enterprise security
blurb: ServiceNow and Accenture partnered to launch a joint enterprise security offering aimed at hardening legacy implementations as agentic AI spreads across more business functions. The tie-up pairs ServiceNow's workflow and security operations tooling with Accenture's deployment services, giving joint customers a single channel to address agent-driven attack surface without assembling the integration themselves.

title: Powering AI agents: CoreWeave’s validation of Nvidia Vera Rubin signals new chapter for rack-scale computing
blurb: CoreWeave and Nvidia completed the first bring-up and validation of the Nvidia Vera Rubin NVL72 rack on CoreWeave Cloud, packing 72 Rubin GPUs, 36 Vera CPUs, and 260TB/s of NVLink 6 bandwidth into a single rack. The validation gives CoreWeave an early position to rent rack-scale capacity for production agentic workloads, ahead of hyperscalers still standing up comparable Rubin infrastructure.

title: Microsoft set for new round of job cuts next week, spanning Xbox, sales and consulting
blurb: Microsoft will cut thousands of jobs next week across Xbox, sales, and consulting, affecting less than 2.5% of its 220,000-person workforce as it reins in operating costs. The reductions land as the company spends more than $100B on AI and cloud infrastructure this fiscal year and its shares sit near a 52-week low, with Wall Street questioning whether the buildout will pay off.

title: Infosec professionals sour on automated pentesting tools
blurb: Security professionals have turned sharply against fully autonomous penetration testing, with the share willing to hand pentests entirely to automated tools falling from 29% last year to 9% now. The reversal signals that early enthusiasm for AI-driven offensive security has run into accuracy and trust limits, and vendors selling autonomous pentesting are likely to face longer procurement cycles as buyers insist on human oversight in the loop.

title: Microsoft keeps Windows Server 2022 hotpatching alive into 2027
blurb: Microsoft extended free hotpatching support for Windows Server 2022 into 2027, delaying the point at which the reboot-free update feature moves behind a paid Azure Arc subscription. IT teams running on-prem 2022 fleets keep the ability to apply security patches without downtime for another year, buying time before they have to weigh the subscription cost against the operational value of skipped maintenance windows.

title: VS Code stops trusting your code by default
blurb: Microsoft shipped VS Code 1.126 with Workspace Trust enabled by default, opening new project folders in Restricted Mode that blocks automatic code execution until a developer explicitly trusts the source. The change hardens the editor against supply-chain and malicious-repo attacks at the endpoint, giving IT security teams a safer default for the millions of workstations running VS Code without a separate policy rollout.

title: Amazon fined $2.25 million for failing to help identity theft victims
blurb: The FTC fined Amazon $2.25 million to settle claims it violated the Fair Credit Reporting Act by refusing to give identity theft victims records of purchases made on fraudulent accounts. The complaint describes support agents withholding transaction details unless victims could name the person who opened the fraudulent account, and Amazon says it has since implemented process improvements for affected customers.

title: What the post-quantum executive order really demands of CISOs
blurb: The author argues the new post-quantum executive order reframes PQC as a readiness and ownership problem for CISOs, not a future cryptography exercise. Federal high-value systems must move key establishment to PQC by Dec. 31, 2030 and digital signatures by Dec. 31, 2031, deadlines that already sit inside enterprise procurement and architecture-review horizons. The more urgent risk is "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" collection, which means long-lived sensitive data is functionally exposed today regardless of the transition timeline.

title: Two thirds of UK enterprises want to ditch US cloud providers – but they're stuck paying a hefty 'sovereignty tax' that keeps them locked in
blurb: A Civo study found 66% of UK enterprises want to leave US cloud providers over digital sovereignty concerns, with 43% saying AI workloads must be hosted in the UK, yet most stay locked into the same hyperscalers they flag as a long-term risk. Civo calls the resulting cost of technical lock-in, migration complexity, and dependency a "sovereignty tax" that buyers are paying without it being a deliberate strategy.

title: Apple accelerates security updates in response to AI-powered hacking risks
blurb: Apple released iOS, iPadOS, and macOS 26.5.2 with security fixes for kernel, WebKit, and WebRTC vulnerabilities that it had originally scheduled for the 26.6 cycle, pulling them forward to shrink the window attackers have to weaponize them. Apple told Reuters the move responds to AI models that now speed the development of hacking tools, even though it found no evidence the patched flaws had been exploited.

title: Apple may struggle to get clearance for Chinese RAM, even for Chinese iPhones
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to buy NAND and DRAM from blacklisted Chinese suppliers CXMT and YMTC, even for iPhones sold only in China. The bid faces long odds, since acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio co-signed a 2022 Senate letter opposing the same YMTC sourcing on supply-chain security grounds, leaving Apple's component diversification strategy exposed to federal-use bans.

title: Meta is reusing old DDR4 RAM in its servers instead of buying new hardware
blurb: Meta built a custom ASIC called Vistara that lets it reuse DDR4 memory from decommissioned servers, bridging the old sticks to newer processors over CXL 2.0 across PCIe Gen5 so retired RAM keeps working in DDR5-based MemServers. The approach closes the gap between Meta's three-to-five-year server refresh cycle and seven-to-ten-year memory lifespan, promising meaningful savings as the company scales the architecture across millions of servers in its AI datacenters.

title: FCC concludes AWS-3 spectrum, raises $3.5B
blurb: The FCC concluded Auction 113, raising over $3.5B from 17 bidders for 200 mid-band AWS-3 licenses that sat idle since EchoStar's Dish Network defaulted on them in 2014. Up to $3.3B of the proceeds will fund the FCC's "rip and replace" program to strip Chinese-made gear from US networks, with an Upper C-band auction of at least 100 MHz due by July 2027.

title: Microsoft MCP server gives AI assistants access to MSBuild logs
blurb: Microsoft introduced the Microsoft Binlog MCP Server, now in preview, which gives AI assistants like GitHub Copilot and Claude Code direct access to MSBuild .binlog files through 15 specialized tools for failure diagnosis, property tracing, and performance analysis. Enterprise .NET teams can query builds in natural language instead of scrolling the Structured Log Viewer, with distribution through the .NET Agent Skills repository across Visual Studio, VS Code, and terminal assistants.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron Sued in US over Memory Price Fixing
blurb: Fourteen consumers and three small businesses sued Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron in California federal court, alleging the DRAM oligopoly colluded since 2022 to cut supply and drive prices up roughly 700% over four years. The plaintiffs claim the trio coordinated the shift to HBM and the discontinuation of DDR3 and DDR4, and if certified as a class action, the case could expose all three to triple damages.

title: How to Govern Autonomous Agents in Enterprise AI Factories
blurb: NVIDIA published its Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design, which moves agent execution off the employee's laptop, browser, or IDE and into a managed VM where identity, credentials, network access, runtime policy, and audit are enforced centrally. The argument for IT leaders is that always-on agents touching internal systems need the same governed perimeter as any privileged workload, with the endpoint reduced to a presentation layer and the VM as the isolation boundary.

title: LeapXpert, which lets companies and sports franchises monitor staff business chats via apps like WhatsApp for compliance, raised $180M led by Riverwood Capital (Lucinda Shen/Axios)
blurb: LeapXpert raised $180M led by Riverwood Capital to expand its platform for monitoring employee business communications across consumer apps like WhatsApp, iMessage, and WeChat for regulatory compliance. The round signals continued enterprise demand for governance tooling as firms in finance, sports, and other regulated sectors face mounting pressure to capture and archive off-channel messaging that traditional compliance systems miss.

title: AI sprawl, token consumption ratchet up tech overspending
blurb: A Flexera survey of 500+ IT asset managers found more than two-thirds lack accurate visibility into AI software usage, and roughly 3 in 5 reported year-over-year AI overspend as wasted SaaS spend climbed 10 percentage points. The report names "tokenmaxxing," the practice of rewarding consumption without governing value, as a driver, citing Uber's burn through its entire 2026 AI budget in four months.

title: Palo Alto, CrowdStrike both have best quarter ever as AI threats bolster cyber demand
blurb: CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks each posted their best quarter on record, rallying 95% and 113% between April and June as AI-driven attack tooling spiked demand for advanced cyber defense. Both anchored the surge on identity and agentic security bets, with Palo Alto closing its $25B CyberArk acquisition and CrowdStrike acquiring SGNL, positioning the two as the default stack enterprises turn to for AI-era protection.

title: Microsoft Plans New Round of Job Cuts as AI and Cloud Spending Tops $100 Billion
blurb: Microsoft plans to cut thousands of jobs as soon as next week across sales, consulting, and Xbox, affecting less than 2.5% of its workforce, as capital spending on AI and cloud infrastructure tops $100B this fiscal year. The round is smaller than last year's 15,000-plus cuts, partly because a first-ever voluntary buyout taken by about a third of 9,000 eligible U.S. staff let Microsoft shrink the layoff share.

title: DHS to unveil replacement council for critical infrastructure cybersecurity
blurb: The Department of Homeland Security is standing up ANCHOR-CI, a CISA-managed council that revives government-industry cyber threat coordination with critical infrastructure operators more than a year after the Trump administration shut down its predecessor, CIPAC. Unlike CIPAC, the new body is exempt from the Federal Advisory Committee Act, so utilities, telecoms, and water and power operators joining its forums will share threat intelligence in meetings closed to public transparency requirements.

title: South Korea’s hot new sensation is 3S+1F – a quadrillion-Won AI plan, not a band
blurb: South Korea unveiled a national AI strategy branded 3S+1F, committing a quadrillion-Won (roughly $730B) public-private push to build sovereign compute, semiconductors, and an AI fund. The plan signals more state-backed demand for GPUs, data-center capacity, and domestic foundation models, positioning Korean vendors like Samsung and Naver against US hyperscalers for regional enterprise workloads.

title: Straiker lands $64M to defend enterprise AI agents from attack
blurb: Straiker raised $64M to expand its agentic security platform, which discovers AI agents running across enterprise systems, tests them for vulnerabilities before deployment, and monitors their behavior at runtime. The round targets a gap legacy rule-based controls miss, a risk Straiker's STAR Labs quantified by finding that 91% of attacks on productivity agents produced silent data theft and 36% of attacks on coding agents reached remote code execution.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Agentic CX designer for Amazon Connect Customer, EC2 AMI Watermarks, Open Governance for MySQL, and more (June 29, 2026)
blurb: Amazon Connect Customer launched Agentic CX designer (NLX) in preview, a no-code canvas that lets business teams design and deploy AI-powered voice and digital self-service flows, combining agentic and deterministic AI in one governed workflow. The release pairs it with Live Sync, which drives a caller's web or mobile screen in real time as they speak, moving CX authoring away from engineering backlogs and toward business users who own the experience.

title: Gemini can now take notes in Google Meet for Google AI Pro and Ultra subscribers.
blurb: Google added a "Take notes for me" feature to Google Meet for AI Pro and Ultra subscribers, with Gemini transcribing calls, generating summaries with action items, and saving them to a Google Doc plus an emailed recap. The feature is host-controlled and also extends to eligible Workspace business customers, notifying all participants when it is enabled.

title: Ford vuelve a contratar a 350 antiguos trabajadores decepcionada con la IA
blurb: Ford rehired roughly 350 veteran quality engineers after its investment in AI and automated quality-control systems failed to catch enough defects, according to Bloomberg. The company isn't dropping AI entirely, but the reversal underscores the limits of replacing experienced inspectors with automation and will have the returning "graybeard" engineers help train junior staff and improve Ford's AI tools.

title: Microsoft adds smarter bot protection to Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft added a Teams Admin Center policy, Manage external bots and their access to meetings, that automatically detects third-party bots, holds them in the lobby, and requires organizer approval before they join. The control gives IT admins visibility over note-takers, transcription tools, and malicious apps in meetings, with allow lists, full-block policies, and audit logs planned as follow-on capabilities.

title: UK regulator wants Apple and Google to let devs steer clear of app store fees
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed rules requiring Apple and Google to let developers steer users toward payment options outside the App Store and Play Store, sidestepping the standard 15% to 30% commissions. The measures, tied to the vendors' new Strategic Market Status designation, would take effect later this year and mirror anti-steering remedies already forcing fee concessions in the EU and US.

title: Verizon and BT intertwine international enterprise efforts
blurb: Verizon and BT Group agreed to merge BT International and Verizon's international enterprise wireline arm into a 50:50 joint venture, with Verizon paying a $625M equalization payment reflecting BT's stronger contribution. The UK-headquartered JV serves over 3,000 customers across 180-plus countries on roughly $4B in combined annual revenue, pitching AI-ready, sovereign connectivity to multinationals and consolidating both carriers' cross-border enterprise operations under one organization led by new CEO Martijn Blanken.

title: Trump budget boss Russell Vought open to re-staffing CISA
blurb: White House budget director Russell Vought told a House Appropriations subcommittee Tuesday that he is willing to work with DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin on re-staffing CISA, after the Trump administration cut more than 1,000 positions from the roughly 3,400-person agency. Mullin wants to add 600 hires, but Vought said he has received no formal request and cautioned that clearances and hiring make the buildout slow to reverse.

title: Updated admin setting for improved video quality in Google Meet
blurb: Google updated the "Limit video bandwidth" admin setting in Google Meet so it now caps both downlink and uplink traffic rather than uplink only, while raising uplink usage for two-person calls to improve quality. Existing configurations carry over automatically with no end-user setting, and the change is rolling out to all Workspace customers starting June 29, 2026.

title: Google Slides can now create entire multi-slide presentations with Gemini
blurb: Google added Gemini-powered generation to Google Slides, letting users build an entire editable presentation from a single prompt, with a planning step, Drive file attachments for context, and existing decks usable as style references. The feature is rolling out now to Business Standard, Business Plus, Enterprise Standard, and Enterprise Plus plans plus Google AI Pro and Ultra tiers, with no administrator controls and higher usage limits through at least August 1.

title: Equinix officially retires bare metal offering
blurb: Equinix officially retired Equinix Metal on June 30, ending the bare-metal business it built from its 2020 Packet acquisition, with console access lingering until December 31, 2026, before all remaining servers are permanently deleted. Customers must migrate or face deletion as rivals Megaport, OVHcloud, and OpenMetal court the departing accounts, while Equinix redirects its colocation focus toward AI-era networking products like its Distributed AI Hub and Fabric Intelligence.

title: Kioxia: Nvidia and AI evolution bank trillions for Japan chip king
blurb: Kioxia is repositioning from an SSD supplier into a full-stack storage partner, leading the Context Memory Extension pillar of Nvidia's Storage-Next effort, which uses SSDs as a memory layer for KV cache and vector databases to ease DRAM and HBM bottlenecks in AI inference. The pivot rode the AI storage boom to make Kioxia Japan's most valuable company this summer, signaling that flash is moving up the AI memory hierarchy alongside HBM rather than staying a commodity tier.

title: Google pitches Spanner as one database for all AI agents with these new featues
blurb: Google Cloud expanded Spanner into a unified data layer for AI agents, folding graph relationships, ScaNN-powered vector search across 10B+ vectors, full-text search, a Cassandra-compatible key-value endpoint, and a columnar analytics engine into one database. Spanner Omni ships the full stack as a containerized build that runs on Kubernetes across Azure, AWS, on-premises, and edge, though Google has not disclosed pricing or general availability.

title: How is AI changing datacenter network fabrics?
blurb: The Register examines how AI training and inference workloads are reshaping datacenter networking, as dense GPU clusters push east-west traffic and lossless, high-bandwidth fabrics to the center of infrastructure design. The piece argues that the old spine-leaf assumptions no longer hold, with 400/800GbE, RoCE, and InfiniBand now gating cluster performance. For IT buyers, the takeaway is that network fabric, not just compute, is becoming a primary constraint and line item in AI buildouts.

title: Japan wants 10 million more robots by 2040, some providing medical care
blurb: Japan's government set a target of adding 10 million robots by 2040, with deployments spanning eldercare, medical support, and disaster cleanup informed by the Fukushima response. The plan pairs the robot rollout with AI to address a shrinking workforce and aging population, signaling a national procurement push that automation and robotics vendors will compete to supply.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: The Git project released Git 2.55.0, adding a `git history fixup` command that amends staged changes into an existing commit and automatically rebases all stacked branches containing it. The release also brings the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without requiring a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: Zuck saves Meta bucks by reusing memory from old servers with a custom CXL ASIC
blurb: Meta built a custom CXL ASIC that pools DRAM salvaged from decommissioned servers, letting newer hosts borrow that memory over the CXL fabric instead of buying fresh DIMMs. The move targets memory cost and e-waste at hyperscale, extending the useful life of RAM that would otherwise be scrapped and easing capacity pressure on memory-bound workloads like caching and inference.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" shipped with systemd-based stage 1 initrd as the default, deprecating the old scripted implementation ahead of its removal in 26.11. The release lands 20,442 new packages and ships GCC 15 and GNOME 50, while marking the final Nixpkgs release to support x86_64-darwin before Apple-driven deprecation ends those builds at the close of 2026.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This CNCF post explains how to combine Linkerd's mesh-derived metrics with an existing OpenTelemetry pipeline, covering the east-west service traffic that application-layer instrumentation misses. Once a workload is meshed, the Linkerd proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request on port 4191, with no SDK calls or image rebuilds. The reference shows where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, plus a working Collector config and Grafana dashboard to land both layers in the same backend.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell that lets servers and edge devices serve browser-based apps over SSH, with each app running as a dependency-free HTTP server bound to a Unix domain socket rather than a localhost port. Encryption is handled at the SSH layer instead of per-app, and apps register capabilities so they can discover each other, turning a remote box into a navigable desktop without a local GUI.

title: Accelerate your infrastructure deployments by up to 4x with AWS CloudFormation Express mode
blurb: AWS announced CloudFormation Express mode, which completes deployments once resource configuration is applied instead of waiting for full stabilization checks, cutting deployment time by up to 4x. AWS benchmarks show an SQS queue with a DLQ dropping from 64 seconds to 10, with CloudFormation auto-retrying dependent resources on transient failures so resources finish stabilizing in the background.

title: Automate public TLS certificate issuance with ACME support in AWS Certificate Manager
blurb: AWS Certificate Manager now runs a fully managed ACMEv2 server endpoint for public certificates, letting Certbot, cert-manager, acme.sh, or any ACME client pull TLS certs from Amazon Trust Services without external CAs. PKI teams get centralized control through IAM-bound ACME accounts, endpoint-level domain scoping, and CloudTrail, CloudWatch, and expiry notifications, ahead of the CA/Browser Forum cutting max certificate validity to 47 days by 2029.

title: Amazon EC2 C9g and C9gd instances powered by AWS Graviton5 processors are now available
blurb: Amazon made its EC2 C9g and C9gd instances generally available, running AWS Graviton5 processors that deliver up to 25% higher performance per vCPU over C8g, DDR5-8800 memory, 5x more L3 cache, and up to 3x faster packet processing than Graviton4. They ship in 11 sizes plus bare metal, with C9gd adding local NVMe SSD storage and free 1-second-granularity I/O latency metrics through CloudWatch or nvme-cli.

title: I ported Kubernetes to the browser
blurb: A developer released webernetes, a partial reimplementation of Kubernetes in TypeScript that runs an entire cluster in the browser, handling pod lifecycles, cluster DNS, IP allocation, container garbage collection, and Deployment/ReplicaSet tracking in roughly 140KiB gzipped. Rather than compiling Kubernetes to WebAssembly, which would ship megabytes and fails on browser-unavailable syscalls, the author rebuilt the control-plane logic and swapped registries for a browser-native image API defined in TypeScript. The project spans nearly 100,000 lines across 629 files, written over two months.

title: Core dump epidemiology: fixing an 18-year-old bug
blurb: OpenAI's data-infrastructure team traced a batch of "impossible" C++ crashes in its Rockset service to two unrelated causes: silent hardware corruption on a single Azure host doing math wrong, and an 18-year-old race condition in GNU libunwind. Rather than chase individual stack traces, the team treated the crashes as a population and built a dataset across every occurrence, letting them separate the two signals. The write-up is a useful model for debugging rare, non-reproducible failures at fleet scale, where per-incident analysis breaks down.

title: Microsoft previews Linux containers that run in Windows
blurb: Microsoft opened a public preview of Linux containers that run natively on Windows, replacing the older Docker Desktop VM approach with a lighter Hyper-V-backed runtime that starts containers faster and shares host resources more efficiently. The change targets platform teams running mixed Windows and Linux workloads, cutting the overhead of maintaining a separate Linux VM per developer machine and tightening the local-to-production parity that container tooling promises.

title: Anomaly detection using dynamic thresholds and two-year-long alerts in Cloud Monitoring
blurb: Google Cloud Monitoring added long-lookback alert policies for PromQL, now in preview, letting policies query up to two years of stored metric data for year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter analysis. The feature enables dynamic thresholds that alert on deviation from a metric's own history, catching anomalies that fall within normal daily ranges and collapsing per-workload static-threshold policies into one.

title: New instances bring AWS’ Graviton5 CPUs to high-performance cloud workloads
blurb: AWS launched its EC2 C9g and C9gd instances, bringing Graviton5 CPUs to compute-intensive workloads like video encoding, scientific modeling, and distributed analytics with up to 25% higher performance per vCPU over Graviton4-based C8g. The chips pair DDR5 8800MT/s memory with 5x the L3 cache of Graviton4, and the C9gd variant adds local NVMe storage for up to 30% higher throughput on I/O-bound jobs.

title: AWS expands compute-optimized Graviton5 instances with C9g and C9gd VMs
blurb: AWS made its compute-optimized Graviton5 instances, C9g and C9gd, generally available in three regions, claiming up to 25% higher per-vCPU performance over the previous C8g line and doubled core counts. Both are the first compute-optimized instances to ship with AWS's Nitro Isolation Engine for mathematically verified VM isolation, with the C9gd adding local NVMe SSD for HPC scratch space and low-latency buffers.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Agentic CX designer for Amazon Connect Customer, EC2 AMI Watermarks, Open Governance for MySQL, and more (June 29, 2026)
blurb: AWS Lambda MicroVMs led this week's roundup, adding a serverless primitive that gives each user or job VM-level isolation on Firecracker with near-instant launch and the ability to suspend and resume execution for up to 8 hours. The same drop shipped EC2 AMI Watermarks, which embed identifiers that carry forward to every derived AMI across copies, Regions, and accounts, plus an Agentic CX designer preview in Amazon Connect for no-code customer-experience flows.

title: etcd-operator joins Cozystack with a new v1alpha2 API
blurb: The etcd-operator project was donated to Cozystack and re-implemented from scratch under a new etcd-operator.cozystack.io/v1alpha2 API, dropping the StatefulSet model in favor of driving etcd's native Membership API for MemberAdd, MemberPromote, and MemberRemove directly. The rewrite reconciles each member's Pod and PVC independently and adds learner-mode members one at a time, giving the operator full control over quorum changes during scaling and graceful removal.

title: Build agents even faster with Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform’s fully-managed, remote MCP server
blurb: Google Cloud launched a fully-managed, remote MCP server for its Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, letting external agents built in Claude Code, Antigravity CLI, or other IDEs securely call Model Garden models, pull shared prompt templates, and manage Notebooks inside a Google Cloud project. Connections run entirely within Google Cloud and are governed by native IAM Deny policies, so IT teams can restrict external frameworks to authorized resources without writing custom integration code.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 on GitLab: More reliable, more efficient
blurb: GitLab made Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 5 available on the Duo Agent Platform across all tiers and deployment models through its AI Gateway, the first model to complete every task in GitLab's internal evaluation suite versus 93.8% for Sonnet 4.6. GitLab reports 8.8% more issues resolved, which for teams running Duo agents in production means multi-file refactors and test generation return reviewable output instead of stalling mid-run.

title: DATADOG rachète ADAPTIVE ML pour renforcer son laboratoire d’IA
blurb: Datadog acquired Adaptive ML, a reinforcement-learning-operations startup, to build out its internal AI research lab and deepen its post-training expertise. The deal is primarily a talent play aimed at turning Datadog's operational telemetry into specialized observability and security models, though the price was undisclosed against Adaptive ML's $20M 2024 raise.

title: HCP Terraform Powered by Infragraph Limited Availability Launch
blurb: HashiCorp moved HCP Terraform powered by Infragraph into limited availability for Standard and Premium customers on US instances, adding connectors for Azure, GitHub, and Terraform Enterprise to the existing AWS support. The tool builds a live graph of resources, relationships, and state across hybrid and multi-cloud environments, giving platform teams a unified view to track ownership, surface risk, and cut the tool sprawl of siloed infrastructure data.

title: How is AI changing datacenter network fabrics?
blurb: The Register examines how AI training and inference workloads are reshaping datacenter network fabrics, driving the shift toward higher-radix switches, 800GbE links, and lossless RDMA-based topologies built for east-west GPU traffic. The piece argues that traditional oversubscribed leaf-spine designs buckle under synchronized all-to-all collectives, pushing operators toward rail-optimized fabrics and tighter congestion control. For platform teams, the takeaway is that network cost and topology now gate cluster utilization as much as GPU supply does.

title: Aikido acquires Root to patch open-source software without forced upgrades
blurb: Aikido Security acquired Root.io to fold its agentic patching into the platform as a feature called Aikido Libraries. Root writes, tests, and ships fixes to vulnerable open-source dependencies at the exact pinned versions teams already run in 15 to 40 minutes, avoiding the rebuilds and breaking changes a full version bump forces.

title: What is AI search optimization? (& why marketers should care)
blurb: AI search optimization, which HubSpot calls answer engine optimization, is the practice of making a brand more likely to be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, and AI Overviews. The traffic is still small but converts well above search: across 1,200-plus publisher sites in a Microsoft Clarity study, AI-referred visitors signed up at roughly 11 times the rate of search visitors, and AI traffic grew 66% in 2025.

title: Instagram is incorporating users' photos in ads for Meta Glasses
blurb: Meta is testing ads for its smart glasses that pull users' own Instagram photos directly into the creative, personalizing each placement with images the viewer already posted. The move signals where Meta is taking ad personalization, pushing marketers to weigh how dynamic, user-content-driven creative performs on CTR and brand trust before opting into formats that blur the line between organic posts and paid placements.

title: Prime Day shows how AI is changing shopping, testing Amazon’s bet against ChatGPT and others
blurb: Shoppers arriving from AI assistants like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini were 40% more likely to buy during Prime Day than those coming from search, email, or social, per Adobe, even as agentic AI still drives under 1% of retail traffic. Amazon is fighting that shift, suing Perplexity and blocking ChatGPT crawlers to keep shoppers on Rufus while quietly buying ChatGPT ads to pull them back.

title: How B2B Buyers Discover You With AI (and Why Your Website Isn’t the Whole Game)
blurb: More than half of B2B software buyers now start product research in ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, or Gemini rather than Google, and 71% use AI somewhere in the process, up from roughly 60% seven months earlier per G2's 2026 survey of 1,000+ buyers. Since models assemble answers from Reddit (nearly 23% of top-cited domains) and YouTube (13%) rather than your site, getting cited across those sources now determines the shortlist.

title: OLIPOP’s Steven Vigilante Says Brands Are Misreading Creator Marketing: ‘One Video’ Is Not a Test
blurb: OLIPOP media lead Steven Vigilante told VidCon 2026 that judging a creator channel on a single video is a broken test, arguing brands should commit $25K to $50K per channel over one to three months before deciding whether it works. The framework helped grow OLIPOP past $400M in annual sales, and the brand now spends more on YouTube than Instagram. Vigilante's premise is that creators who actually use the product integrate it into content people choose to watch, outperforming interruptive ad formats.

title: Popular design trends that destroy conversion
blurb: Abstract hero visuals, the now-ubiquitous gradient blobs and light-leaks, are tanking conversion because the highest-leverage spot on the page communicates nothing in the two seconds visitors take to decide. The author argues this is herd behavior: teams copy a generic "landing page blueprint" out of FOMO rather than testing it. The fix is a concrete hero that names the product, the value, and the offer instead of decorative ambiguity.

title: Meta is adding ridiculous ‘rate limits’ and a soft paywall to its smart glasses
blurb: Meta is limiting the Conversation Focus feature on its AI glasses to three hours per month unless owners pay $19.99 for a Meta One Premium subscription, even though the feature runs entirely on-device without touching Meta's servers. The move signals Meta's push to monetize already-purchased hardware as it absorbs heavy AI investment costs, testing how much recurring revenue it can extract from a device install base.

title: Gemini can now take notes in Google Meet for Google AI Pro and Ultra subscribers.
blurb: Google Meet now offers "Take notes for me" to Google AI Pro and Ultra subscribers, with Gemini transcribing calls in real time, generating a summary with action items, and saving it to a Google Doc plus an emailed recap. Meeting hosts enable it per-call via the pencil icon or default-on in settings, and all participants get notified when note-taking is active.

title: TikTok launches Agentic Hub
blurb: TikTok launched Agentic Hub, a centralized library of first- and third-party AI Skills wired to its Model Context Protocol server so AI agents can run campaign creation, creative generation, catalog management, and performance analysis inside its ad platform. HubSpot, Wix, Constant Contact, and Mobvista have already published Skills, giving advertisers pre-built connectors to automate manual campaign work and pull optimization recommendations from real engagement data.

title: YouTube Shorts Dislike Data Stops Updating by Month’s End as Platform Overhauls Viewer Feedback Layer
blurb: YouTube will stop updating Shorts dislike counts in YouTube Studio by the end of June, pointing creators instead toward Likes and a views-to-swipe-away ratio as the primary performance signals. The platform is also adding reason-specific input to the "Not Interested" option to feed its recommendation engine, so swipe-away rate, not dislikes, now drives short-form distribution.

title: Podcasting platform Riverside enters the newsletter publishing game
blurb: Riverside added a newsletter tool that uses AI to convert users' existing videos and podcasts into newsletter-ready drafts sendable from within its app. Rather than competing head-on with Substack or Beehiiv, the play targets creators who already record spoken content, alongside new multi-camera recording, remote guests, and AI-generated social hooks.

title: Consumers trust AI for discovery but not for finding deals
blurb: Similarweb found that AI chatbots are increasingly used in the early discovery phase of shopping journeys, while consumers switch back to traditional search engines for price comparison and deal-hunting later in the funnel. The split means marketers should treat AI assistants as a top-of-funnel awareness channel and keep optimizing product listings and retargeting for the search-driven, high-intent moments closer to purchase.

title: WhatsApp prompts users to choose a username ahead of update
blurb: WhatsApp is prompting its roughly 3B users to claim a unique username, fronted by a promotional campaign with boxer Anthony Joshua, ahead of an update that makes the handle a primary ID instead of a phone number. The shift opens a more private contact layer for brands, letting businesses run messaging and support without surfacing customer phone numbers and reshaping how WhatsApp fits into outreach playbooks.

title: How A Branded Subreddit Helps Cursor Own BoFu Discussions
blurb: Cursor built an owned presence in r/cursor with two dozen-plus employee accounts, and the 143,000-member community now ranks for 482 keywords, 107 of them in the top three, pulling roughly 9,600 organic visits a month worth about $13,000 in equivalent traffic. Because developers vet usage-based tools through peer threads rather than vendor pricing pages, Cursor answers billing and roadmap questions directly on the channel that ranks above its own site for bottom-of-funnel queries. The tradeoff is ceding control of the narrative to a community that can turn on the roadmap.

title: Google Slides can now create entire multi-slide presentations with Gemini
blurb: Google Slides now generates entire multi-slide presentations from a single Gemini prompt, with a planning step that asks about audience and tone and lets you edit the outline before slides are built. Marketers can attach Drive files or an on-brand deck as a style reference, and every generated slide stays fully editable; the feature is English-only and live on Business Standard and up plus Google AI Pro and Ultra.

title: Meta adds custom Instagram Stories features for glasses
blurb: Meta added custom Instagram Stories features to its AI-powered Ray-Ban glasses, letting wearers capture and post multi-camera and motion-capture formats directly from the device. The move gives brands and creators a new native format to test on Instagram, opening hands-free, first-person Stories content as glasses adoption grows.

title: TikTok launches branded microdramas
blurb: TikTok launched branded microdramas, letting companies produce and promote their own episodic short-form series as ad inventory riding the platform's fast-growing microdrama trend. The format gives brands a native way to sustain attention across multiple beats rather than a single 15-second spot, opening a new placement to test against standard in-feed video on CPM and completion rate.

title: Google wiped out 45% of AI content in one update: Here’s what the survivors did differently
blurb: Google's March 2024 core update folded helpful-content signals into the main ranking algorithm and, over a 45-day rollout, cut an estimated 45% of low-quality, unoriginal pages from search, hitting sites that published AI content at scale with minimal editorial oversight hardest. By mid-2025, teams that had over-invested in AI production were reallocating toward editorial work: using AI upstream for user research rather than draft generation, and enforcing human editing downstream before anything ships.

title: Bad data is teaching AI to waste your ad budget
blurb: Automated bidding now acts on your conversion data before you ever review it, so a double-fired tag or mislabeled conversion value doesn't just distort a dashboard, it trains Smart Bidding to chase the wrong customers. Google reads only the numeric value on each conversion event and optimizes toward it immediately, with no understanding of where that action sits in your funnel. Verifying conversion accuracy now protects budget more than tweaking ad copy does.

title: How ChatGPT adoption has expanded
blurb: OpenAI's new Signals data shows ChatGPT usage deepening as well as spreading, with users sending 50% more daily messages and doubling their distinct task types six months after signup. The fastest relative growth is in Africa, Asia, and lower-HDI countries, users with typically feminine names now account for most global usage, and non-English usage is climbing, signaling that the addressable AI audience skews younger and more global than most channel plans assume.

title: Using Large Language Models as Low-Cost Statistical Estimators for Human-Response Data
blurb: This paper proves that under squared loss, a well-calibrated LLM can match the Bayes-optimal risk for any inference that depends on data only through the conditional mean, making it a low-cost stand-in for human-subject experiments. The authors formalize the LLM as a misspecified estimator, decompose its error into representation bias and optimization error, and provide finite-sample bounds plus a calibration protocol with explicit decision rules. For PMs, it sets out the conditions under which synthetic user data can substitute for real research, and where it cannot.

title: YouTube Shorts Dislike Data Stops Updating by Month’s End as Platform Overhauls Viewer Feedback Layer
blurb: YouTube will stop updating Shorts dislike counts in YouTube Studio by the end of June, pointing creators instead toward Likes and a views-to-swipe-aways ratio as the primary performance signals. The change adds a sub-layer to the viewer "Not Interested" option, where specific dismissal reasons now feed the recommendation engine, so PMs tracking Shorts performance will need to rebuild dashboards around engagement metrics rather than dislikes.

title: 39 principles for designing human-AI interaction
blurb: The author distills research from human-AI interaction, mixed-initiative systems, and trust-in-automation into 39 applied principles for designing AI interfaces that support appropriate reliance, user control, and transparency. The core argument is that product quality isn't set by model capability alone: the same model can feel useful or dangerous depending on when the system suggests versus acts, how it surfaces uncertainty, and what evidence backs a generated answer. Newer principles pull directly from OpenAI's Model Spec and Anthropic's Claude Constitution on honesty and preserving human oversight.

title: Sonnet 5 review: I ran 64 generations to find out if it's worth it
blurb: Claire Vo built a repeatable eval harness, the How I AI Bench, and ran Sonnet 5 blind against Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3 Pro across PRD quality, prototype generation, agentic tasks, and agent personality. Scores combined human vibe rating (70%) with LLM-as-judge (30%), and the takeaway is a per-task recommendation rather than a single winner, so PMs know which model to reach for by job.

title: Great Products, Bad Companies
blurb: Marty Cagan argues that great products are necessary but not sufficient for great companies, because a proven product-making company becomes an attractive target for people who infiltrate boards, replace missionary founders, and strip-mine the culture for a fast financial win. He points to Eric Ries's new book Incorruptible as a reason to stop treating that fate as unavoidable, and to start designing governance that protects long-term value the way product teams protect customer trust.

title: Circle craters 17% as Stripe, Coinbase and BlackRock back rival stablecoin network
blurb: Circle (CRCL) shares fell more than 17% Tuesday to a four-month low after a consortium of over 140 companies, including Stripe, Coinbase, Mastercard, Visa and BlackRock, launched Open USD to challenge USDC. The rival stablecoin lets partners mint and redeem without fees and keep reserve income minus a management fee, targeting the core economics that make incumbent issuers profitable.

title: OKX and Coinbase Race to Sign Up Binance's Displaced EU Users Before MiCA Deadline
blurb: OKX and Coinbase launched competing deposit-bonus campaigns to capture EU users losing access to Binance, which will restrict services across the bloc starting July 1 after withdrawing its MiCA license application in Greece. OKX is offering tiered USDC rewards up to 8% on deposits to $500,000, while Coinbase is targeting Coinbase One subscribers in seven countries, both running through July 13.

title: BNY Adds USDC to Institutional Custody Platform in Expanded Circle Partnership
blurb: BNY, the largest custodian bank with $59.4T under custody, made USDC the first stablecoin on its Digital Asset Custody platform, letting institutional clients store, transfer, mint, and redeem Circle's token alongside traditional assets in one environment. The move follows BNY's January tokenized-deposit launch and the GENIUS Act, which lets federally chartered banks custody payment stablecoins, with BNY planning to add more issuers over time.

title: BlackRock Adds Ethena's Synthetic Dollar to Its $20T Aladdin Risk Management Platform
blurb: BlackRock added Ethena's USDe synthetic dollar as an approved digital asset on Aladdin, its institutional risk platform spanning more than $20T in assets, opening the $4.45B-supply token to the banks, insurers, and pension funds that run money on the system. The deal also makes BlackRock's tokenized Treasury fund BUIDL the primary backing for Ethena's whitelabel stablecoins, with Ethena committing a $100M liquidity facility for BlackRock's tokenized products.

title: Taiwan passes key crypto law, clearing legal uncertainty for digital asset sector
blurb: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan passed the Virtual Asset Service Act on its third reading, requiring crypto exchanges and stablecoin issuers to secure Financial Supervisory Commission licenses and imposing criminal penalties of up to seven years and NT$100M ($3.14M) for unlicensed operation. Existing AML-registered platforms get 12 months to apply for a license and 21 months to obtain FSC approval, while stablecoin issuers must hold full reserves and clear both the central bank and FSC.

title: Visa, Stripe, Coinbase and more join Open USD stablecoin that shares reserve revenue
blurb: Visa, Stripe, Coinbase, and other payments firms joined Open Standard's Open USD, a stablecoin that shares reserve revenue with the businesses using it. Set to launch later this year, Open USD will let companies mint and redeem the token without fees or volume limits, positioning it against incumbents like Circle's USDC and Tether's USDT.

title: Strategy Authorizes up to $1.25B of Bitcoin Sales as Saylor Formalizes Capital Pivot
blurb: Strategy's board approved a "Digital Credit Capital Framework" authorizing up to $1.25B in Bitcoin sales to fund dividends, interest, and buybacks, formalizing that the largest corporate BTC holder may now sell from its 847,363 BTC position. The pivot reverses Saylor's accumulate-and-hold identity as the stack sits roughly $14B underwater and STRC preferred stock trades well below its $100 target, straining the share-sale funding model.

title: 236,000 DCloud Uni-App Sites Used in Crypto Scams, Phishing, and Wallet Drainers
blurb: Infoblox identified 236,493 distinct domains running investment-scam templates built on DCloud Uni-App, a legitimate Chinese cross-platform framework, powering fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering operations, phishing networks, and wallet drainers since mid-2022. The set includes RainbowEx, the bogus exchange behind a 2024 Ponzi scheme that hit tens of thousands in San Pedro, Argentina, and led to seven arrests.

title: Securitize Shareholders Approve Merger, Paving Way for First Publicly Traded Tokenization Company
blurb: Securitize shareholders approved its merger with Cantor Equity Partners II, clearing the final hurdle for the tokenization platform to list on the NYSE under ticker SECZ on July 2 as the first publicly traded tokenization company in the US. The SPAC deal delivers roughly $400 million in gross proceeds, including a $225 million PIPE, to a firm that runs BlackRock's BUIDL fund and holds over $4 billion in assets under management.

title: Circle shares sink 16% after Open USD reveal, analysts say fears are ‘overblown’
blurb: Circle shares fell more than 16% on Tuesday after Open Standard announced Open USD, a new stablecoin backed by over 140 companies including Visa, Stripe, Mastercard, BlackRock, and Coinbase. William Blair reiterated its Outperform rating and called the selloff overblown, pointing to USDC's roughly $74B market cap, deep liquidity, and the Circle Payments Network as advantages new entrants will struggle to replicate.

title: Taiwan’s sweeping crypto law raises the bar with licensing, reserve mandates, and tough penalties
blurb: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan passed the Virtual Asset Service Act, requiring all crypto exchanges and service providers to obtain Financial Supervisory Commission licenses and meet stricter custody, cybersecurity, and governance standards. Stablecoin issuers must secure central bank and FSC approval while holding 100% reserves, and unauthorized operation carries up to seven years in prison and NT$100M ($3.14M) in penalties.

title: Visa, Mastercard and 140 firms launch Open USD, a stablecoin built to undercut Circle
blurb: A consortium of 140+ firms including Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, Coinbase, BlackRock and Google launched Open USD, a dollar-pegged stablecoin that lets businesses mint and redeem at no cost and routes reserve interest to partners rather than a single issuer. The model targets Circle's core revenue source, and Circle shares fell 15% to 17% on the announcement.

title: Trump pocketed more than $1 billion from crypto ties as industry headed toward slump
blurb: Trump earned more than $1 billion from crypto in 2025, per his federal financial disclosure, including $635 million in memecoin royalties and over $500 million from World Liberty Financial token sales. The gains landed as the broader market slumped, with bitcoin down roughly 50% from its late-2025 high and World Liberty still facing conflict-of-interest complaints while it pursues US regulatory approvals.

title: MetaMask unveils all-in-one Money Account offerings users up to 4% APY on mUSD holdings
blurb: MetaMask launched Money Account, an all-in-one product that pays users up to 4% APY on holdings of its mUSD stablecoin. Consensys named Monad as the account's home chain and will initially route yield through Morpho vaults, integrating the feature directly into the wallet rather than a separate DeFi app.

title: MetaMask launches Money Account with stablecoin yield and spending in one wallet
blurb: MetaMask launched Money Account, a self-custodial product built on Monad that combines up to 4% stablecoin yield, MetaMask Card spending, and trading in a single account. Yield accrues on its mUSD stablecoin through automated allocation to lending protocols including Morpho, with Aave integrations planned, and balances stay usable for swaps, perps, and prediction markets without transfers.

title: Open Standard Unveils Open USD, a Bank- and Tech-Backed Stablecoin Governed by Its Users
blurb: A consortium of more than 140 companies including Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, BlackRock, Coinbase, Ripple and Google introduced Open USD (OUSD), a dollar stablecoin that routes nearly all reserve earnings and governance to adopting businesses rather than a single issuer. OUSD launches natively on Solana with no minting fees or volume caps, though reserve custodians, attestation cadence and the regulated issuing entity remain undisclosed ahead of its 2026 launch.

title: Visa, Stripe and 140 others back new Open USD stablecoin to challenge Tether
blurb: A consortium of more than 140 companies including Visa, Stripe, BlackRock, Mastercard, and Coinbase backed Open USD, a new stablecoin operated by Open Standard LLC that launches later this year with no mint or redemption fees and reserve revenue shared with partners. The fee-sharing model targets Tether and Circle, which hold roughly 62% and 25% of stablecoin supply, and Circle shares fell about 13% on the announcement.

title: JPMorgan's Kinexys Blockchain Hits $4 Trillion, Adds Five APAC Currencies
blurb: JPMorgan's Kinexys blockchain crossed $4 trillion in cumulative transactions and added the Australian dollar, Hong Kong dollar, Japanese yen, Chinese renminbi, and Singapore dollar to its Blockchain Deposit Account network, bringing the total to eight currencies. The permissioned platform now averages over $7 billion in daily volume and extends 24/7 settlement to APAC trade corridors, with Payoneer and JERA Global Markets among the first regional clients.

title: President Trump’s financial disclosures show hundreds of millions in crypto-related income
blurb: Trump's federal financial disclosure reported over $236M in token-sale proceeds and $65.6M from equity sales tied to World Liberty Financial, the DeFi venture backed by his family. The filing also detailed cold-wallet holdings of BTC, ETH, USDC, LINK, AAVE, ENA, MOVE, and ONDO, plus roughly $1.8M in ether staking rewards, as lawmakers debate ethics provisions in pending crypto legislation.

title: Spot bitcoin ETFs record worst month since debut, shedding $4.5 billion in June
blurb: US spot bitcoin ETFs recorded $4.5B in net outflows in June, their worst month since launching in January 2024 and 29% above the prior record set in February 2025. BlackRock's IBIT drove $3.55B of the total, with analysts attributing the withdrawals to macro-driven capital rotation and SpaceX's $75B IPO pulling fresh dollars out of crypto rather than any shift in bitcoin fundamentals.

title: SEC opens ETF rule review following crypto fund surge, prediction markets push
blurb: The SEC opened a public comment period on how it regulates "novel ETFs," including crypto funds and a proposed class tied to prediction markets on political and economic outcomes. The review follows dozens of altcoin ETF approvals under Chair Paul Atkins, and TD Cowen expects it could clear a broader array of event-contract, crypto, and single-stock ETFs as soon as 2027, with comments due in 60 days.

title: DraftKings Launches Proprietary Prediction Markets Exchange With $3.4B Annualized Consumer Volume
blurb: DraftKings launched DKeX, a proprietary prediction markets exchange reporting roughly $3.4B in annualized consumer volume and $11.3B in total trading volume for the week ended June 21, with the 2026 FIFA World Cup cited as a key demand driver. The CFTC-licensed platform, built on its acquired Railbird Technologies, gives DraftKings full ownership of its predictions stack and immediate distribution to sports bettors across 30 states as it challenges Polymarket and Kalshi.

title: Kalshi hit with 14-day restraining order in Michigan, blocking sports prediction markets in state
blurb: A Michigan judge granted state gaming regulators a 14-day temporary restraining order against Kalshi, halting the platform's sports prediction markets in the state while the case proceeds. The order adds to a widening jurisdictional fight in which Kalshi argues its CFTC registration preempts state authority, even as gaming regulators across multiple states move to block its event contracts.

title: Crypto firms have spent $189M so far on 2026 US election, report says
blurb: Crypto firms have spent $189M on the 2026 US midterm elections so far, according to a report on political donations tracked ahead of November. The total, concentrated in industry-backed super PACs like Fairshake, positions the sector among the largest corporate political spenders and signals a continued push to shape favorable regulatory outcomes.

title: SEC wins $5.5 million default judgment over alleged fake crypto platform NanoBit
blurb: A federal judge in New York entered a $5.5M default judgment against NanoBit Limited and five related defendants over a "pig-butchering" scam that used a fake crypto trading platform to defraud at least 18 investors of nearly $1M. The SEC alleged the operators built trust through WhatsApp groups, displayed fabricated trading dashboards, and wired user funds to Hong Kong bank accounts instead of executing any trades.

title: Crypto firms in the UK face stricter FCA rules
blurb: The FCA published its final rules bringing UK crypto firms under financial-services standards, requiring trading platforms, custodians, stablecoin issuers, and staking arrangers to obtain authorisation and meet capital, stress-testing, and market-integrity requirements covering insider trading and manipulation. Stablecoins get dedicated standards with simplified capital requirements after consultation, though the full regime does not take effect until October 2027, leaving FCA oversight limited to promotions and anti-money-laundering controls until then.

title: Silent Swap Crypto Clipper Uses Fake Google Notes Extension to Replace Wallet Addresses
blurb: McAfee Labs detailed Silent Swap, an active clipper campaign that installs a fake "Google Notes" Chromium extension to swap copied wallet addresses for attacker-controlled ones mid-transaction. The writeup breaks down its two notable techniques: EtherHiding, which stores C2 resolver details in a smart contract so operators can rotate domains without redeploying malware, and covert extension injection that rewrites protected browser preference files across Chrome, Edge, Brave, and Vivaldi. Because on-chain transfers are irreversible, a single swap means permanent loss.

title: CFTC Expands Polymarket Probe to Staged Trades and Fake Wins
blurb: The CFTC expanded its Polymarket investigation to cover staged trades and fabricated winning bets, extending a probe that began as an influencer advertising-disclosure inquiry into market-conduct territory, per Bloomberg. The scrutiny follows a bipartisan Senate letter citing a WSJ finding that Polymarket paid creators to film roughly $1.9M in fake wagers on dummy sites, with a July 10 response deadline set for the agency.

title: SEC giving novel ETFs a rethink as it opens comment period on overhauling U.S. rules
blurb: The SEC opened a 60-day request for comment on overhauling its rules for novel exchange-traded funds, including crypto, single-stock, and event-contract products, questioning how such funds qualify for streamlined listing. Analysts read the move as groundwork for policy that would let ETFs hold a broader asset universe, extending a framework that grew the US ETF market from $4T in 2019 to $12T in 2025.

title: ENS co-founder Nick Johnson blocks Security Council renewal with 80% of votes
blurb: ENS co-founder Nick Johnson blocked the renewal of the protocol's Security Council with roughly 80% of governance votes, citing unresolved concerns with the current structure. Johnson backed an alternative proposal submitted Tuesday, leaving ENS without a confirmed council slate as the DAO weighs the competing plan.

title: New Crypto Bill Draft Expected Soon
blurb: Senate Republicans plan to release an updated draft of their industry-backed crypto market structure bill soon, with revisions aimed at addressing members' concerns over ethics provisions. The move signals continued momentum toward comprehensive US digital-asset regulation, though the ethics rework suggests remaining friction among lawmakers before the measure can advance to a floor vote.

title: Jefferies warns of crypto market volatility as Clarity Act faces Senate test
blurb: Jefferies warned that the Clarity Act's uncertain path through the Senate will fuel elevated volatility across crypto tokens and blockchain-related equities in the coming weeks, with roughly 20 legislative days left before the August recess. Polymarket now puts the odds of passage by end-2026 at 48%, down from 70% in mid-May, as ethics provisions, illicit-finance concerns, and limited floor time weigh on the bill.

title: OKX AI unveils marketplace for agents to find work and get paid in stablecoins
blurb: OKX launched OKX AI, a beta agentic marketplace where autonomous AI agents list services, post tasks, and settle payments in USDT or USDG stablecoins via escrow contracts or instant pay-per-call transactions. Each agent carries a single persistent identity that accrues reputation across jobs, and the launch is backed by the Ethereum Foundation, the Solana Foundation, Opentensor Foundation, AWS, CertiK, and StraitsX.

title: Securitize Set to Go Public Thursday
blurb: Securitize debuts on the New York Stock Exchange Thursday following investor approval of its SPAC merger, making it one of the first publicly traded pure-play tokenization companies. The listing gives public markets direct exposure to the real-world asset tokenization sector, where Securitize administers tokenized funds including BlackRock's BUIDL.

title: Phantom doubles down on perpetual futures with hire of Hyperliquid market builders
blurb: Phantom hired Alvin Hsia, Emily Hsia, and Aris Samad, the team behind Ventuals, the Hyperliquid project that ran onchain perpetual futures on OpenAI and Anthropic valuations before winding down this week. The trio will join Phantom's trading and data teams as the self-custody wallet pushes deeper into perpetuals, extending a shift from asset storage into swaps, staking, and derivatives.

title: FCA reveals ‘landmark’ crypto rules
blurb: The FCA published its final regulatory framework for UK crypto firms, requiring trading platforms, custodians, intermediaries, stablecoin issuers, and staking providers to obtain authorization and meet capital, stress-testing, and market-integrity standards covering insider trading and manipulation. Stablecoin rules, issued jointly with the Bank of England, take effect in October 2027, with systemic issuers moving to joint FCA-Bank supervision once designated by HM Treasury.

title: Self-exiled Chinese billionaire sentenced to 30 years for fraud involving crypto
blurb: Guo Wengui, the self-exiled Chinese billionaire arrested in 2023, was sentenced to 30 years in prison for orchestrating a fraud conspiracy of more than $1 billion that involved cryptocurrency. Prosecutors said Guo used a web of media ventures and crypto offerings, including the GTV and Himalaya Exchange tokens, to defraud his online followers out of their funds.

title: Apify lets AI Agents pay via Coinbase x402 for web tools
blurb: Apify integrated Coinbase's x402 payment protocol, letting autonomous AI agents discover, pay for, and run its web-automation Actors with USDC micropayments on Base, no account or API key required. The launch makes roughly 20,000 pay-per-result Actors callable over x402, pushing the protocol's tool count from about 2,000 to over 20,000, with a dollar covering around 350 Google Maps listings per run.

title: Why OpenUSD's 'real threat' that tanked Circle stock still faces a steep uphill battle for adoption
blurb: Circle shares (CRCL) fell 16% on Tuesday after the Open Standard consortium, backed by more than 140 companies including Stripe, Coinbase, Visa, Mastercard, and BlackRock, launched Open USD, a stablecoin that distributes reserve yield to partners rather than keeping it like USDC. Analysts called the selloff an overreaction, noting that Paxos' consortium-backed USDG has reached only $3B in supply and that OUSD still faces open questions on structure and adoption.

title: Pump.fun's PUMP Buybacks Top $400M as Token Stays Flat
blurb: Pump.fun has repurchased and burned over $400M of its PUMP token across 346 days, removing roughly 145.5 billion tokens from circulation under the 50%-of-revenue buyback policy it adopted in April. The burns have yet to lift the price, with PUMP down about 16% over the past 30 days and trading roughly 83% below its September record as memecoin fee generation cools to $23.5M monthly.

title: Bitcoin's correlation with dollar-yen rate hits -0.90, undercutting 'carry trade' theory
blurb: Bitcoin's 52-week rolling correlation with USD/JPY has fallen to -0.90, its most negative reading since late 2022, meaning roughly 81% of weekly BTC moves now track the dollar-yen rate inversely. The pattern undercuts the carry-trade narrative that a weakening yen lifts risk assets, and likely reflects broad dollar strength tied to shifting Fed rate expectations rather than a direct BTC-yen link.

title: Sharplink buys 10,000 ETH, repurchases 2.13 million SBET shares in latest buyback
blurb: SharpLink bought 10,000 ETH at an average of $1,611 per token, raising its treasury to 886,725 ETH as of June 28 in its first disclosed ether purchase in eight months. The company also repurchased 2.13 million SBET shares under its buyback program, lifting cumulative repurchases to 4.07 million shares since August 2025 following last week's $75 million registered direct offering.

title: Shanghai court sentences five to prison over $29 million crypto forex scheme
blurb: A Shanghai court sentenced five people to prison for running a $29 million scheme that used cryptocurrency to facilitate illegal foreign exchange transfers. Chinese authorities uncovered the operation in July 2024 after flagging unusual transactions tied to a company moving funds overseas, reinforcing Beijing's continued enforcement against crypto-enabled capital flight despite its domestic trading ban.

title: Magic Eden, Founders Sued by $ME Buyers Over Broken 'Utility' Promises
blurb: Three $ME buyers sued Magic Eden and its four co-founders in federal court in New York, alleging the company promoted use cases like multichain trading, governance, and staking rewards, then delayed or abandoned them. The class action brings New York consumer-protection claims rather than securities claims, testing whether a token's marketed utility can support recovery for holders; $ME trades near $0.056, down roughly 99% from its high.

title: Chinese exile once linked to Trump strategist gets 30-year sentence in $1 billion fraud
blurb: A U.S. judge sentenced Chinese businessman Miles Guo to 30 years in prison for a set of interrelated fraud schemes that stole more than $1 billion, including Himalaya Coin, an H-Coin token he marketed as 20% gold-backed with a guarantee against investor losses. Guo, a China exile with close ties to former Trump strategist Steve Bannon, was ordered to forfeit nearly $900 million in proceeds following his 2024 conviction on racketeering, fraud, and money laundering counts.

title: Don't See Slowdown in Institutional Client Interest: Markova
blurb: Fireblocks policy director Dea Markova said institutional client interest in crypto shows no signs of slowing, speaking on Bloomberg Crypto ahead of an expected updated draft of the Clarity Act. The revised market-structure bill would clarify how digital assets are classified between the SEC and CFTC, a jurisdictional question that has kept many institutional allocators on the sidelines.

title: Phantom Hires Ventuals Founders After Hyperliquid Perps Venue Winds Down
blurb: Phantom, the largest Solana wallet by market share, hired the three-person team behind Ventuals, the Hyperliquid-based pre-IPO perpetuals venue that wound down in mid-June after more than $650M in lifetime volume. The acqui-hire, which excludes Ventuals' product, deepens Phantom's derivatives push after it added Hyperliquid-powered in-wallet perps in July 2025 and expanded into equity and pre-IPO markets.

title: Visa, Mastercard and 140 firms launch Open USD, a stablecoin built to undercut Circle
blurb: A consortium of 140+ firms including Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, Coinbase and BlackRock launched Open USD (OUSD), a dollar-pegged stablecoin run by independent company Open Standard that lets businesses mint and redeem at no cost and routes most reserve interest to partners rather than a single issuer. The model directly attacks Circle's reserve-income economics, and Circle shares fell 15-17% on the news despite Tether and USDC still holding roughly 87% of the market.

title: OKX and Coinbase Race to Sign Up Binance's Displaced EU Users Before MiCA Deadline
blurb: OKX and Coinbase launched competing deposit-bonus campaigns to capture European users losing access to Binance, which restricts EEA services on July 1 after withdrawing its MiCA license application in Greece. OKX, licensed in Malta, is offering up to 8% on transfers, while Coinbase is targeting Coinbase One subscribers across seven markets, both exploiting MiCA's licensing requirement as a structural moat against unauthorized rivals.

title: BNY Adds USDC to Institutional Custody Platform in Expanded Circle Partnership
blurb: BNY, the world's largest custodian with $59.4T in assets under custody, made USDC the first stablecoin supported on its Digital Asset Custody platform, letting institutional clients hold, transfer, mint, and redeem Circle's token alongside traditional assets. The integration, enabled by the GENIUS Act's custody authority for chartered banks, collapses the handoff between traditional custodian and stablecoin-infra provider into one operational framework, with BNY planning to add more issuers over time.

title: BlackRock Adds Ethena's Synthetic Dollar to Its $20T Aladdin Risk Management Platform
blurb: BlackRock will list Ethena's USDe synthetic dollar as an approved digital asset on Aladdin, its risk platform managing over $20T for banks, insurers, and pension funds, while BUIDL becomes the primary reserve for Ethena's whitelabel stablecoins. The tie-up is the deepest link yet between the largest asset manager and a DeFi derivatives protocol, signaling traditional finance is moving capital onchain rather than building rival rails.

title: Visa, Stripe, Coinbase and more join Open USD stablecoin that shares reserve revenue
blurb: Visa, Stripe, Coinbase, and others joined Open Standard's Open USD, a stablecoin that shares reserve revenue with its participating members. Expected to launch later this year, Open USD lets businesses mint and redeem the token without fees or volume limits, a direct challenge to the reserve-yield economics that USDC and Tether keep for themselves.

title: Taiwan passes key crypto law, clearing legal uncertainty for digital asset sector
blurb: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan passed the Virtual Asset Service Act in its third reading, requiring crypto exchanges and stablecoin issuers to obtain a license from the Financial Supervisory Commission before operating, with stablecoin issuers also needing central bank approval and full reserve backing. Existing AML-registered platforms get 12 months to apply and 21 months to secure approval, and violators face up to seven years in prison and NT$100M ($3.14M) fines.

title: Strategy Authorizes up to $1.25B of Bitcoin Sales as Saylor Formalizes Capital Pivot
blurb: Strategy's board approved a Digital Credit Capital Framework authorizing up to $1.25B in Bitcoin sales to fund dividends, interest, and buybacks, formalizing that the largest corporate BTC holder may now sell from its 847,363 BTC stack. The move codifies a capital pivot for a company built on never selling, arriving as its STRC preferred stock trades below target and its Bitcoin position sits roughly $14B underwater.

title: Securitize Shareholders Approve Merger, Paving Way for First Publicly Traded Tokenization Company
blurb: Securitize Corp. begins trading on the NYSE July 2 under ticker SECZ after Cantor Equity Partners II shareholders approved the SPAC merger, making it the first publicly traded tokenization company in the US. The deal delivers roughly $400M in gross proceeds, including a $225M PIPE, giving the BlackRock BUIDL issuance platform a public balance sheet to expand regulated digital-securities infrastructure across the US and EU.

title: Circle craters 17% as Stripe, Coinbase and BlackRock back rival stablecoin network
blurb: Circle shares fell more than 17% to a four-month low after Open Standard, a consortium of over 140 companies including Stripe, Coinbase, Mastercard, Visa and BlackRock, launched Open USD, a stablecoin that lets partners retain reserve income and mint without fees. The design strikes at USDC's core economics, shifting stablecoin competition from token issuance to who controls the underlying network and captures the float.

title: MetaMask unveils all-in-one Money Account offerings users up to 4% APY on mUSD holdings
blurb: MetaMask launched Money Account, an all-in-one wallet feature that pays users up to 4% APY on holdings of its mUSD stablecoin. Consensys chose Monad as the product's home chain and will route yield through Morpho vaults initially, folding on-chain lending returns directly into a self-custody wallet interface.

title: Taiwan’s sweeping crypto law raises the bar with licensing, reserve mandates, and tough penalties
blurb: Taiwan's Legislative Yuan passed the Virtual Asset Service Act, requiring all crypto exchanges and platforms to obtain FSC licensing and meet stricter custody, cybersecurity, and fund-segregation standards, with stablecoin issuers needing central bank and FSC approval plus 100% reserves. Unlicensed operation now carries penalties of up to seven years in prison and NT$100M (about $3.14M), moving Taiwan from basic AML registration to full regulatory supervision once President Lai signs.

title: Trump pocketed more than $1 billion from crypto ties as industry headed toward slump
blurb: Trump earned more than $1B from crypto sales and royalties in 2025, per his Office of Government Ethics disclosure, including $635M in memecoin royalties and over $500M from World Liberty Financial token sales. The gains came while he pursued aggressively pro-crypto policies and as the broader market slumped, with bitcoin down roughly 50% from its fall high and World Liberty still seeking U.S. regulatory approvals amid conflict-of-interest complaints.

title: Circle shares sink 16% after Open USD reveal, analysts say fears are ‘overblown’
blurb: Circle shares fell more than 16% on Tuesday after Open Standard unveiled Open USD, a stablecoin backed by over 140 companies including Visa, Stripe, Mastercard, BlackRock, and Coinbase, raising investor fears of a direct challenge to USDC. William Blair called the selloff overblown, reiterating its Outperform rating and pointing to USDC's ~$74B market cap, deep liquidity, and Circle's payments network as defenses new entrants will struggle to replicate.

title: Open Standard Unveils Open USD, a Bank- and Tech-Backed Stablecoin Governed by Its Users
blurb: A consortium of more than 140 companies, including Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, BlackRock, BNY and Coinbase, introduced Open USD (OUSD), a dollar stablecoin that returns nearly all reserve earnings to the businesses that adopt it rather than to a single issuer. Governed collectively through Open Standard and launching natively on Solana in 2026, OUSD inverts the incumbent float-income model, though its reserve custodians, attestation cadence and issuing entity remain undisclosed.

title: MetaMask launches Money Account with stablecoin yield and spending in one wallet
blurb: MetaMask launched Money Account, a self-custodial product from Consensys that lets users earn up to 4% variable yield on stablecoins, spend via the MetaMask Card on Mastercard rails, and trade from a single balance. Built on Monad and centered on the mUSD stablecoin, it auto-routes deposits to lending protocols like Morpho, positioning wallet providers as full financial platforms rather than storage tools.

title: Visa, Stripe and 140 others back new Open USD stablecoin to challenge Tether
blurb: A consortium of more than 140 companies, including Visa, Stripe, BlackRock, Mastercard, and Coinbase, is backing Open USD (OUSD), a new stablecoin from Open Standard LLC launching later this year to challenge Tether and Circle. The token lets businesses mint and redeem with no fees or volume limits and returns most reserve revenue to partners, breaking from the issuer-keeps-the-interest model that made USDT and USDC dominant.

title: JPMorgan's Kinexys Blockchain Hits $4 Trillion, Adds Five APAC Currencies
blurb: JPMorgan's Kinexys blockchain crossed $4 trillion in cumulative transactions and added the Australian dollar, Hong Kong dollar, Japanese yen, Chinese renminbi, and Singapore dollar to its permissioned Blockchain Deposit Account network, extending 24/7 settlement to APAC trade corridors.
